subject: Pediatrics
title: Pediatric fractures
preambleMarkdown:
# Pediatric fractures

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatric fractures is a clinical condition or syndrome in pediatrics.
- This article focuses on age-specific presentation, growth, development, family counseling, weight-based dosing, and safety.
- The practical clinical priority is pediatrics diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Pediatric fractures to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatric fractures is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is pediatrics diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Pediatric fractures depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In pediatrics, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Pediatric fractures.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Pediatric fractures disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Pediatric fractures should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Pediatric fractures first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Pediatric fractures, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Pediatric fractures, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused pediatrics examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Pediatric fractures presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Pediatric fractures with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Pediatric fractures is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Pediatric fractures should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Pediatric fractures is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses pediatrics diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Pediatric fractures, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Pediatric fractures may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Pediatric fractures patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Pediatric fractures, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Pediatric fractures in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Pediatric fractures, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Pediatric fractures does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Pediatric fractures may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Pediatric fractures, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Pediatric fractures as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Pediatric fractures draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Pediatric Trauma

_Source lines: 33681-33843._

```text
XI. PEDIATRIC TRAUMA (SEE ALSO CHAPTER 15)
A. Discussion. Trauma accounts for approximately one-half of deaths in the pediatric
age group. The initial assessment of children proceeds as with the adult, first com-
pleting a primary survey and then the secondary survey (see Clinical Pearl 17-7).

B. Clinical features
1. Shock can be rapidly fatal to a child. Children have extensive compensatory
capabilities, and a drop in BP is a late and ominous sign, not occurring until
there has been 25% to 30% of blood volume loss.

a. Tachycardia is the primary response to hypovolemia in children.

Tachycardia may also be associated with pain and anxiety in children.

b. Tachypnea. RR may also be increased to compensate for metabolic acidosis
secondary to decreased tissue perfusion.

c. Delayed capillary refill. Capillary refill may be delayed (longer than
2 seconds) if there is decreased perfusion and shock.

2. Head injury is common in the pediatric population and accounts for approxi-
mately 50% of accidental deaths, with motor vehicle accidents being the lead-
ing cause.

a. Scalp lacerations may cause extensive blood loss in children, and pressure
should be applied to control bleeding.

b. Most skull fractures in children are linear and may have no associated
symptoms except local tenderness. Depressed skull fractures usually result
from direct blunt trauma and may be open. Basilar skull fractures include
fractures in the basal portion of the frontal, temporal, and occipital bones,
and ethmoid and sphenoid fractures. Findings are the same as those seen in
the adult.

c. Epidural and subdural hematomas are relatively uncommon and occur
more frequently in older children. Diagnosis and management principles are
the same in child as in adults. Approximately 75% of these children con-
tinue to have seizures.

3. Spinal cord injury is uncommon in children, accounting for approximately 5%
of all spinal cord injuries seen.

Quick 	HIT
Complete anterior and poste-
rior urethral injuries require
surgical repair.

Quick 	HIT
The Broselow tape is a great
resource when treating in-
jured children.

Quick 	HIT
Children may cry from fear
as well as pain, and they may
be less able to localize their
pain than adults.

Quick 	HIT
Early recognition of hypovo-
lemia to prevent shock can-
not be overly stressed.

Quick 	HIT
Head injury is the leading
cause of trauma deaths in
children.

Quick 	HIT
Fractures are seen in only
approximately 50% of pedi-
atric patients with epidural
hemorrhages.

Quick 	HIT
More children than adults,
though, may have spinal cord
injury without radiographic
abnormality.

Differences between Children and Adult Trauma Victims
1. A child's head occupies a relatively larger proportion of body surface area than an adult's. Bony sutures
fuse by 18 to 24 months. A child's brain has a larger proportion of unmyelinated fibers, making it more
susceptible to shear injury.

2. Children have shorter necks with less muscle support and more cartilage.

3. The larynx is more cephalad and anterior, located at approximately C3-C4 in the infant (C4-C5 in
the adult). In children younger than 8 years, the subglottic area at the cricoid ring is the narrowest part of
the upper airway (in adults, it is at the level of the vocal cords). It is this area that is the limiting factor in
determining endotracheal tube size.

4. The chest is much more compliant with less bony structure in the chest wall, more cartilage, less over-
lying protective muscle mass, and a more horizontally placed and distensible diaphragm.

5. The mediastinum is more mobile and more susceptible to a tension pneumothorax.

6. The abdominal wall is thinner with less protective muscle and fat. The liver, spleen, and bladder
(which is an intraperitoneal structure in children) are all more susceptible to injury.

7. Children have a higher proportion of body surface area to body mass and are more subject to heat
loss and hypothermia.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	17-7
T R A U M A T I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	413

Traumatic Emergencies
C. Evaluation
1. Primary and secondary survey. The evaluation of the pediatric trauma patient
should proceed in a systematic fashion as in adults.

2. Diagnostic imaging studies
a. CT is the preferred imaging modality in moderate and severe injuries. Due to
the cancer risks from excessive radiation, judicious use should be the rule.

The "pan-scan" philosophy in all trauma cases is potentially dangerous.

b. Ultrasonography is a valuable screening tool for abdominal and thoracic injuries.

D. Treatment
1. Initial stabilization
a. Fluid resuscitation. Children become hypothermic quickly, and this could
negatively affect outcome. Warming lights, blankets, and warmed intrave-
nous fluids should be used in all cases.

i. Normal saline or Ringer lactate (20 mL/kg bolus over 10 minutes) is the
initial fluid of choice. If the patient fails to respond, a repeat bolus of
20 mL/kg may be given.

ii. If there is no response to crystalloid, packed red blood cells (RBCs) are
given at 10 mL/kg.

b. Airway
i. Rapid sequence induction (see Chapter 1.III.B.3) should be done when
intubating a trauma patient.

a) Atropine is given (0.02 mg/kg, minimum dose 0.1 mg) to prevent
bradycardia secondary to vagal stimulation.

b) Neuromuscular blockade
1) A defasciculating dose of a nondepolarizing paralytic is not
required in young children.

2) The dose of succinylcholine is higher in children (2 mg/kg) as
opposed to adults and children (1 to 1.5 mg/kg) older than 10 to
12 years.

ii. Endotracheal intubation. Endotracheal tube size may be estimated
using the following formula: (16 " age in years) % 4. Uncuffed endotra-
cheal tubes are used in patients younger than 6 to 8 years.

2. Management principles for chest, abdomen, and head injuries are the same in
children as in adults.

E. Disposition. Children with minor head injury and no loss of consciousness, nor-
mal neurologic examination, and a reliable caretaker may be discharged. Many chil-
dren with isolated orthopedic injuries may also be treated and discharged home.

Patients with any other significant injury require admission to a general pediatric
floor or to an ICU; some patients may require transfer to a pediatric trauma center.
```

### Source Excerpt: Pediatric Orthopedics

_Source lines: 19841-20227._

```text
PEDIATRIC ORTHOPEDICS
I. General Principles
A. History and physical
l . In all pediatric patients , it is important to obtain a birth and developmental
history. Specifically, gestational age at birth, breech position in utero , age
of crawling, age of walking, and family history of developmental delay or
difficulties.

## 2 . Remember that children are not just small adults from a musculoskeletal

standpoint. They have signiHcant changes with bony and soft-tissue growth
throughout childhood. From a skeletal standpoint, pediatric patients have
growth plates (physes) that can affect length and alignment of bones if injured.

B. Common pediatric orthopedic problems include developmental disorders ,
anatomic variants , genetic disorders, and trauma.

II. Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip DDH1
A. Introduction
l . Abnormal development of the hip secondary to soft-tissue laxity and uterine
positioning may present with frank hip dislocation.

2. Risk factors include breech position, female gender, family history, and first born.

The left hip is most commonly affected because of intrauterine positioning.

3. Treatment and prognosis change significantly if the diagnosis is made after
6 weeks of age .

B. History and physical
l . A family and personal delivery history of breech position must be taken.

2. A physical exam is extremely important for detecting hips that may be affected.

A focused exam of the hip should be performed on every newborn. Any test
abnormalities below warrant a further workup :
a. Ortolani maneuver: reduction of a dislocated hip with femur elevation and
abduction ( "hip clunk")
b . Barlow maneuver: subluxation or dislocation of the affected hip with ad
duction and posteriorly directed force
c. Galeazzi sign: clinical appearance of a shortened femur on the affected side
d. Asymmetrical abduction of the affected hip when compared to contralateral side

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 2 7 5
Proper positioning o f an i nfant i n a Pavl ik harness. The harness i s composed of
shoulder straps, sti rrups, and a chest strap. It is placed on both legs, even if o n ly
one h i p is dislocated.

I
( R e p rinted with permission from Klossner N J , H atfield NT. Introductory MatePiV & Pediatric Nursing. 2nd e d . P h i l a d e l p h i a , PA:
Li p p i n c ott Williams & Wilkins; 2009. 1
C. Diagnosis
1 . Physical exam should make a practitioner highly suspicious for DDH.

## 2 . Diagnosis is conHrmed via dynamic ultrasound demonstrating an unreduced

or dislocated hip . Because the femoral head does not ossify until 4 to 6 months
of age , plain radiographs may not be helpful during this time period.

D . Treatment
1 . Treatment is focused on obtaining and maintaining a concentric hip reduction.

The treatment course depends on the patient age at diagnosis .

a. Zero to 6 weeks: Pavlik harness (Fig. 1 5-7)
b . Six weeks to 1 year: A Pavlik harness is applied if hip is reducible; other
wise, closed or open reduction with possible soft-tissue releases followed
by casting is performed.

Ill. Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis lSCFEI
A. Introduction

## 1 . Pathology is displacement of proximal femoral epiphysis posteriorly and

medially relative to metaphysis.

2 . This is seen in children ages 10 to 15 years who are commonly obese, male ,
and African-American. Up to 25% of cases may be bilateral.

## 3 . Younger patients ( < 1 0 years old) and bilateral cases are more commonly

associated with an underlying endocrine abnormality (e.g. , hypothyroidism,
renal failure , growth hormone deficiencies) .

B . History and physical
1 . Most common complaint may be knee pain secondary to referred pain.

All children with knee pain require a radiograph of the hip . Also, patients
commonly present with limited rotation of the hip or a limp.

2. On physical exam, all patients have obligatory external rotation with flexion of
the hip . They also commonly have limited hip rotation. Pain may be present in
groin, thigh, or knee.

C . Diagnosis

## 1 . AP pelvis and frog-leg lateral radiographs of bilateral hips will show displace

ment of the femoral head epiphysis through the physis. If concerned for bilat
eral slips, consider an endocrine workup (Fig. 1 5-8) .

2. Radiographic Hndings may be subtle in early slips. Often, the only abnormality
may be seen on the lateral view of the affected hip. It is helpful to compare the
affected hip to the unaffected hip on the same image.

S C F E is c o m m o n i n o b e s e
a d o l e s c e nts w i t h h i p o r
k n e e p a i n . T h e fe m o r a l n e c k
m o v e s rel ative to the f e m o r a l
e p i p hysis t h r o u g h the p h y s i s .

0 1 ( g e n etic c o l l a g e n d i s e a s e )
patie nts have b l u e s c l e r a e
a n d s u s t a i n m u lt i p l e low- )e n e rgy fractures.

(Reprinted with p e rmission from Swiontkowski M F, Stovitz S D , eds. Manual of OQhopaedics. 6th e d . P h i l a d e l p h i a , P A : Lippincott Wil
liams & Wilkins; 2006.)

3. Loder classiHcation
a. Stable: The patient is able to weight bear with or without crutches.

b. Unstable: The patient is unable to weight bear secondary to pain.

D . Treatment
l . SCFE is treated with operative intervention.

## 2 . Usually, in situ percutaneous pinning of the femoral neck and head is

preferred.

IV. Osgood-Schlatter Disease
A. Introduction
l . This is an overuse disease most common in males that usually presents in late
childhood or early adolescence.

## 2 . This results from a partial avulsion of the anterior portion of the tibial tuber

osity through the apophysis . The cause is recognized as a juvenile traction
osteochondrosis of the tibial tuberosity due to stress from the knee extensor
mechanism (patellar tendon) .

B. History and physical
l . Patients often present with a history of knee pain that is worse with activity
and often participate in sports. They may complain of a prominence or
"bump" on the front of their knee.

2 . Physical exam demonstrates a tender prominence over the tibial tuberosity.

C. Diagnosis
l . Diagnosis is often made by history and physical alone. If pain is severe, may
consider obtaining AP and lateral plain radiographs of the knee and tibia
2. Lateral radiograph may show avulsion and fragmenting of the tibial tubercle.

D. Treatment
l . The disorder is usually self-limiting.

2. Often, decreasing activity, resting, and taking anti-inflammatories helps the
symptoms .

3 . Occasionally, the avulsion will not heal after the patient has Hnished growth.

If painful, this area may be excised surgically.

V. Osteogenesis lmperfecta 011
A. Introduction
l . OI is a skeletal dysplasia caused by a defect in type I collagen that causes
abnormal cross-linking of collagen and "brittle bones . "

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 2 7 7

## 2 . Common clinical findings include bowing o f long bones, multiple fractures,

short stature, scoliosis, hearing loss, blue sclera , and tooth defects .

3 . There are multiple types of 0 1 , with different inheritance patterns.

B. History and physical
l . Family history is important to obtain because many of the types of this
disorder have an inheritance pattern, usually autosomal dominant or
autosomal recessive .

2. A history of multiple Xactures in childhood, sometimes present at birth, is a
hallmark. Fractures tend to cease at puberty.

3. Physical exam may show short stature, bowing of lower extremities, and
scoliosis. Check the eyes for blue sclera and mouth for tooth defects.

C. Diagnosis
l . Diagnosis is made based on clinical presentation and physical exam findings.

Often, family history is positive .

2. Radiographs may show history of multiple fractures. The bones tend to have
thin cortices and be diffusely osteopenic.

3. A fracture of the olecranon process in pediatric patients is pathognomonic for OI.

D. Treatment
l . Treatment includes management of fractures , correcting scoliosis, and
improving function.

2. Often, surgical procedures are required for Xacture stabilization. These can
become quite complicated secondary to the deformity of the long bones and
the fact that they must continue to allow for growth of the child.

VI. Septic Arthritis
A. Introduction
l . Septic arthritis occurs in children, most often under 3 years of age. However,
it can occur in any age . The hip is the commonly affected j oint.

2. Most commonly caused by hematogenous spread; however, it may spread
from a bone infection (osteomyelitis) when the metaphysis of the bone is
intra-articular (hip , elbow, ankle, or shoulder) .

## 3 . Common organisms (by age of patient)

a. < l2 months   group B Streptococcus, Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus
influenzae
b. 6 months to 5 years   Haemophilus inKuenza, Staphylococcus aureus
c. 5 to l2 years   Staphylococcus aureus
d. l 2 to 18 years L Staphylococcus aureus, Neisseria gonorrhoeae
B. History and physical
.-
l . Patient will present with fever, pain , and limping or inability to bear weight on
the affected limb . With young infants, pain with diaper changes or decreased
movement of the auected limb may be the only clues present.

2. Common physical exam findings include fever, pain (especially with range of
motion of the joint) , limping, or decreased use of the affected limb.

C. Diagnosis
l. The Kocher criteria are helpful to evaluate patients to diagnose septic hip arthritis.

2. These criteria are ( l ) temperature > 1 0 1 .5 F, (2) white blood cell (WBC)
count > 1 2 ,000, (3) erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) >40 mm/hr, and
(4) inability of the patient to weight bear (see Clinical Pearl 1 5-5) .

CLIN ICAL PEARL 1 5-5
Kocher Criteria for Septic Arthritis
Number of Criteria Met
4/4
3/4
2/4
1 /4
Chance That the Child Has Septic Arthritis
99%

	t
U s e the K o c h e r c rite ria to
c o rrectly d isti n g u i sh s e ptic
a rthritis and transient
synovitis of the h i p .

Staphylococcus au reus i s
m o st c o m m o n b a cteria f o r
j o i n t a n d b o n e infectio n s .

A:

3 . Ultrasound can be useful to help evaluate for the presence of a j oint effusion.

If present, aspiration should be performed to obtain a fluid sample for labora
tory analysis . joint fluid analysis findings consistent with septic arthritis are:
a. WB C > 5 0 ,000/mm3
b. Decreased glucose
c. Increased protein
d. Positive gram stain
D. Treatment
l . Septic arthritis in children is a surgical emergency. Pus is chondrolytic and
destroys cartilage.

## 2 . Emergent irrigation and debridement of the affected j oint is performed in the

operating room, followed by a course of IV antibiotics .

VII. Salter-Harris Fractures
A. Introduction
l . Fractures in the long bones of children can injure the growth plate (physis)
near the ends of the bones. If the fracture damages the growth plate , growth
arrest can occur. This can lead to an angular deformity (if only a portion of
the growth plate is damaged) or, in some cases , complete growth arrest. In the
lower extremity, this leads to leg length discrepancies.

## 2 . Correctly identifying and classifying growth plate injuries allows for a detailed

discussion with the patient and the family regarding expected prognosis and
complications.

B. History and physical
l . History and physical exam are usually consistent with a traumatic injury. Pain,
deformity, and swelling are common with fractures. Remember to examine for
open wounds.

C. Diagnosis
l . Like in adult fractures, AP and lateral views of the involved area are required
to diagnose the injury. Remember to image the entire bone as well as the j oint
above and the j oint below.

2. ClassiHcation of pediatric fractures is by the Salter-Harris classification
(Fig. 1 5-9) . It is based on the amount of the growth plate involved and
location of other fracture lines:
a. Type I: fracture through the physis
b. Type II: fracture through the physis and the metaphysis, sparing the epiphysis
c. Type III: Xacture through physis and epiphysis, sparing the metaphysis
d. Type IV: fracture through all three elements of the bone (physis, metaphysis,
and epiphysis)
e. Type V: compression fracture of the physis, resulting in a decrease in the
perceived space between the epiphysis and metaphysis
D. Treatment
l . Fractures that are nondisplaced are usually treated conservatively with casting.

Displaced fractures that can be reduced may also be treated conservatively.

F I G U R E
Salter- Harris classification of physical fractures.

I I I I I I I V
( R e p rinted with p e rmission from Stri c k l a n d JW, G r a h a m TJ , eds. Master Techniques in Orthopaedic Surgery: The Hand. 2 n d e d .

P h i l a d e l p h i a , P A : Li p p i n c ott Williams & W i l k i n s ; 2005. )

O R T H O P E D I C S U R G E R Y e 2 7 9

## 2 . Fractures that involve the articular surface (types I I I and IV) require anatomic

reduction via surgical intervention, speciHcally ORIF. Irreducible fractures also
require ORIF.

VIII. Child Abuse [Nonaccidental Trauma]
A. Introduction
l . As a physician, it is state law to report suspected child abuse.

## 2 . Fractures in children younger than 2 to 3 years of age should raise suspicion,

especially lower extremity fractures in children not yet walking.

B. History and physical
l . A history of multiple visits to the emergency department should raise
suspicion. Also, a story told by the caregiver that does not seem to fit the
circumstances should raise red flags .

2. Exam Hndings can include multiple Xactures at various stages of healing,
multiple healing bruises, skin marks (hand prints) , burns, malnutrition, and
other signs of neglect.

C. Diagnosis
l . The most common locations of fractures in child abuse are humerus, tibia,
and femur. For suspected cases, a skeletal survey can be useful to evaluate for
multiple old and new Xactures.

2. Common Xacture patterns in child abuse are metaphyseal (corner) and spiral
fractures. However, any fracture in children could be from abuse.

D . Treatment
l . Notification to child protective services is mandatory for any suspected cases of
child abuse. If abuse is missed:
a. One in three children will have further abuse .

b . There i s a 5% t o 1 0% chance of death i n these children.

## 2. Routine Xacture care
```

- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
